refined_fr.refined_fr: The NGHE solution shall maintain teleoperation communication packet loss at or below the customer-defined threshold (acceptance criterion: ≤1% under agreed network conditions) to ensure safe and effective remote equipment operation.
refined_fr.rationale: CR-NGHE-009 addresses a critical quality-of-service requirement for teleoperation. Packet loss directly impacts command latency, feedback fidelity, and operator situational awareness—all safety-critical factors in remote heavy equipment control. Refinement clarifies that the requirement is conditional on 'agreed network conditions' (a boundary condition) and ties acceptance to a measurable, customer-negotiated metric rather than a fixed absolute.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Conduct packet loss measurement under simulated and live network conditions matching the agreed profile (e.g., 4G/5G coverage, latency budget, interference scenarios). Measure end-to-end packet loss for telemetry uplink and tasking downlink separately. Validate that loss remains ≤1% across the agreed operational envelope.
sub_frs: FR-NGHE-009.1: The teleoperation system shall transmit operator commands (tasking) with packet loss ≤1% under agreed network conditions.
FR-NGHE-009.2: The teleoperation system shall receive equipment telemetry with packet loss ≤1% under agreed network conditions.
FR-NGHE-009.3: The teleoperation system shall detect and report communication quality degradation (packet loss approaching or exceeding threshold) to the operator and safety services.

candidate_dps: DP-A: Implement redundant communication channels (dual-path routing) with automatic failover to maintain packet loss below threshold.
DP-B: Deploy adaptive forward error correction (FEC) and packet retransmission logic to recover lost packets within latency budget.
DP-C: Implement network quality monitoring and dynamic codec/compression adjustment to reduce payload size and improve transmission reliability.
DP-D: Combine redundant channels (DP-A) with adaptive FEC (DP-B) and quality monitoring (DP-C) to achieve defense-in-depth packet loss mitigation.
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP-D: Implement a defense-in-depth communication resilience strategy combining (1) redundant dual-path routing with automatic failover, (2) adaptive forward error correction and selective retransmission within latency budget, and (3) continuous network quality monitoring with dynamic payload optimization. This multi-layer approach addresses packet loss at the transport, codec, and routing levels, providing robustness across diverse network conditions while maintaining operator command latency within safety-critical bounds.

potential_sources: Coupling between redundant channel management (DP-A) and FEC logic (DP-B): both compete for bandwidth; mitigation via priority queuing and latency budgeting.
Coupling between quality monitoring (DP-C) and codec adaptation: codec changes must not introduce jitter or command latency spikes; mitigation via staged transitions and operator notification.
Coupling with Safety Services: quality degradation alerts must integrate with SafetyAlert interface; mitigation via formal interface contract.
coupling_check.notes: DP-D is a composite design that decouples concerns via layered responsibility: routing layer handles redundancy, codec layer handles compression/FEC, monitoring layer handles adaptation. Couplings are manageable through interface contracts and latency budgeting. No fundamental contradiction detected.

assumptions_risks: Assumption: Dual-path routing is available in the agreed network conditions (e.g., 4G + 5G, or terrestrial + satellite). Risk: If only single-path network is available, redundancy cannot be realized; mitigation: validate network topology in customer agreement.

Assumption: Latency budget for retransmission is ≥100 ms (typical for heavy equipment teleoperation). Risk: If safety-critical commands require <50 ms round-trip, retransmission may not be viable; mitigation: define latency budget explicitly in requirements.

Assumption: Operator and Safety Manager accept dynamic codec switching. Risk: Unexpected video quality changes may reduce operator confidence; mitigation: provide explicit operator control and gradual transitions.
Risk: FEC overhead increases bandwidth consumption; if network is bandwidth-constrained, FEC may paradoxically increase loss. Mitigation: adaptive FEC that scales with observed loss rate.
selected_dp.recommended_revision: Refine DP-D to explicitly define (1) latency budget for retransmission (e.g., max 150 ms round-trip), (2) redundancy topology (e.g., 4G primary + 5G secondary), (3) FEC overhead cap (e.g., max 20% bandwidth increase), and (4) codec adaptation thresholds (e.g., switch to lower quality if loss >0.5%). This will reduce ambiguity and enable clearer IM assignment and verification.

im_boundary.name: Teleoperation
im_boundary.scope: The Teleoperation module owns the end-to-end communication resilience strategy for remote equipment operation, including redundant channel management, adaptive FEC/retransmission, network quality monitoring, and dynamic payload optimization. It interfaces with the Vehicle module (for telemetry ingestion and tasking injection) and Safety Services (for quality alerts). It abstracts the underlying Network Link from higher-level teleoperation logic.

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Ingests equipment state (position, sensor readings, diagnostics) from Vehicle module. Teleoperation module monitors packet loss on this uplink and applies FEC/retransmission as needed. Expected frequency: 10–50 Hz depending on equipment type.

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: Delivers operator commands (movement, tool control, mode changes) to Vehicle module. Teleoperation module ensures tasking downlink packet loss ≤1% via redundancy and FEC. Expected frequency: 1–20 Hz depending on operator input rate.

external_interfaces.name: SafetyAlert
external_interfaces.details: Reports communication quality degradation (packet loss approaching threshold, channel failover events, codec adaptation) to Safety Services. Enables proactive operator notification and emergency shutdown if needed.

external_interfaces.name: Network Link
external_interfaces.details: Abstracts the underlying physical/wireless network (4G, 5G, satellite, terrestrial). Teleoperation module manages dual-path routing, failover logic, and quality monitoring at this layer. Network Link provides raw packet transmission and loss statistics.

module_containment_scores.notes: NGHE Solution is the top-level system container. Assigning DP-D to NGHE Solution would diffuse the communication resilience logic across multiple sub-modules (Vehicle, Teleoperation, Safety Services) without clear ownership. Not recommended as primary IM boundary.

Vehicle module owns equipment state and actuation. While it generates telemetry and consumes tasking, the communication resilience logic (redundancy, FEC, monitoring) is orthogonal to vehicle control and should not be embedded in Vehicle. Assigning DP-D to Vehicle would couple communication concerns with vehicle dynamics, reducing modularity.

Teleoperation module is the natural owner of end-to-end communication quality. It sits between the operator interface and the Vehicle/Safety Services, making it the ideal location for redundancy, FEC, quality monitoring, and adaptation logic. High containment: all DP-D elements (routing, FEC, monitoring) are cohesive within teleoperation concerns. Low diffusion: no need to scatter logic across other modules.

Safety Services owns safety policy enforcement and alerts. While it consumes SafetyAlert from Teleoperation, it should not own the communication resilience logic itself. Assigning DP-D to Safety Services would blur the boundary between communication quality and safety policy. Not recommended.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: The Teleoperation module is the optimal IM boundary for DP-D because (1) it owns the logical flow of telemetry and tasking between operator and vehicle, (2) communication resilience (redundancy, FEC, monitoring) is a core concern of teleoperation, not a cross-cutting concern, (3) it has clear interfaces to Vehicle (Telemetry, Tasking) and Safety Services (SafetyAlert), and (4) it abstracts the underlying Network Link, allowing independent evolution of network technology. Containment score 0.95 reflects high cohesion and low diffusion.
containment_risks: Risk: If Teleoperation module becomes too large (>10K LOC or >5 major sub-components), it may need further decomposition. Mitigation: plan sub-level decomposition of Teleoperation into redundancy manager, FEC codec, quality monitor, and payload optimizer.

Risk: Network Link interface may be owned by a separate infrastructure module (e.g., Connectivity). If so, Teleoperation must depend on Network Link as an external service, not own it. Mitigation: clarify Network Link ownership in system architecture; ensure Teleoperation can query loss statistics and request failover.

Risk: Safety Services may need real-time access to packet loss data for safety-critical decisions (e.g., emergency stop if loss >5%). If Teleoperation buffers or delays SafetyAlert, safety response may be compromised. Mitigation: define SafetyAlert latency budget (e.g., <100 ms) and ensure Teleoperation publishes alerts synchronously.

Risk: Operator interface (tele-operator console) may be in a separate module. Teleoperation must provide clear feedback on communication quality to the operator. Mitigation: define operator feedback interface (e.g., quality indicator, codec status) as part of Teleoperation's external contract.
assigned_im.recommended_revision: Confirm that Network Link is a separate, owned module (or external service) and that Teleoperation has a well-defined interface to query loss statistics and request failover. Define SafetyAlert latency budget and operator feedback interface explicitly. Plan sub-level decomposition of Teleoperation into (1) Redundancy Manager, (2) FEC Codec, (3) Quality Monitor, and (4) Payload Optimizer to manage complexity and enable independent testing.

fr_dp_gate.reason: DP-D (defense-in-depth communication resilience) directly addresses FR-NGHE-009 (packet loss ≤1%). The multi-layer approach (redundancy, FEC, monitoring) is necessary and sufficient to achieve the acceptance criterion under diverse network conditions. No logical gap between requirement and design.

diffusion_risks: Redundancy logic (dual-path routing, failover) may require coordination with Network Link module. Ensure Teleoperation has clear API to request failover and query path status.

FEC codec may need to be shared with other modules (e.g., video streaming for operator feedback). Mitigation: implement FEC as a reusable library within Teleoperation, with clear interface for external consumers.

Quality monitoring may generate high-frequency telemetry (loss rate, latency, jitter). Ensure Teleoperation does not create a bottleneck by publishing too much data. Mitigation: aggregate and filter quality metrics before publishing to SafetyAlert.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: Teleoperation module has high containment (0.95) for DP-D. All core logic (redundancy, FEC, monitoring) is cohesive within teleoperation concerns. External dependencies (Network Link, Vehicle, Safety Services) are well-defined via interfaces. No significant diffusion risk.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: Verification of FR-NGHE-009 is feasible via Teleoperation module. Packet loss can be measured end-to-end by instrumenting Telemetry and Tasking interfaces. Quality monitoring logic within Teleoperation can log loss statistics for analysis. Acceptance testing can simulate network conditions (packet drop, latency, jitter) and validate that loss remains ≤1%.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: Allocation is clear: Teleoperation module owns the implementation of DP-D and is responsible for meeting the packet loss acceptance criterion. Vehicle module provides telemetry and consumes tasking (no packet loss responsibility). Safety Services consumes SafetyAlert (no packet loss responsibility). Network Link provides raw transmission (no packet loss responsibility; Teleoperation compensates via FEC/redundancy).

recommended_revisions: Refine DP-D to specify latency budget for retransmission (e.g., max 150 ms round-trip) and FEC overhead cap (e.g., max 20% bandwidth increase). This will enable clearer verification criteria.
Define SafetyAlert latency budget (e.g., <100 ms) to ensure Safety Services can respond to communication degradation in time.
Plan sub-level decomposition of Teleoperation module to manage complexity and enable independent testing of redundancy, FEC, and monitoring logic.
Clarify Network Link ownership and API (e.g., how Teleoperation requests failover, queries loss statistics, configures redundancy).
Define operator feedback interface (e.g., quality indicator, codec status) to ensure tele-operator is aware of communication quality and adaptation events.

decision.justification: FR-NGHE-009 is not atomic and has been refined into three sub-FRs (command transmission, telemetry reception, quality reporting). DP-D is a composite design that combines redundancy, FEC, and monitoring—each of which requires further decomposition into concrete algorithms and data structures. Teleoperation module is the correct IM boundary, but its internal structure (redundancy manager, FEC codec, quality monitor, payload optimizer) must be designed at the next level. Proceeding to sub-level decomposition is necessary to achieve sufficient detail for implementation and verification.
decision.justification_detail: The refined FR-NGHE-009 has three sub-FRs that address distinct concerns: (1) tasking downlink reliability, (2) telemetry uplink reliability, and (3) quality monitoring/alerting. DP-D is a high-level strategy that must be decomposed into concrete sub-DPs for each concern. For example, redundancy for tasking may differ from redundancy for telemetry (tasking is command-critical, telemetry is feedback-critical). FEC parameters (code rate, block size) must be tuned for each data stream. Quality monitoring thresholds must be calibrated to the operator's situational awareness needs. Proceeding to sub-level decomposition will clarify these distinctions and enable detailed design and verification.
next_level_sub_frs: FR-NGHE-009.1: The teleoperation system shall transmit operator commands (tasking) with packet loss ≤1% under agreed network conditions.
FR-NGHE-009.2: The teleoperation system shall receive equipment telemetry with packet loss ≤1% under agreed network conditions.

FR-NGHE-009.3: The teleoperation system shall detect and report communication quality degradation (packet loss approaching or exceeding threshold) to the operator and safety services within 100 ms of detection.

assumptions: Dual-path routing is available in the agreed network conditions (e.g., 4G + 5G, or terrestrial + satellite).
Latency budget for retransmission is ≥100 ms (typical for heavy equipment teleoperation).
Operator and Safety Manager accept dynamic codec switching with explicit notification.
Network Link is a separate, owned module with API for failover and loss statistics.
SafetyAlert latency budget is <100 ms to enable timely safety response.
Teleoperation module will be further decomposed into sub-modules (Redundancy Manager, FEC Codec, Quality Monitor, Payload Optimizer).
open_risks: If only single-path network is available, redundancy cannot be realized. Mitigation: validate network topology in customer agreement.
If safety-critical commands require <50 ms round-trip, retransmission may not be viable. Mitigation: define latency budget explicitly in requirements.
FEC overhead may increase bandwidth consumption and paradoxically increase loss if network is bandwidth-constrained. Mitigation: adaptive FEC that scales with observed loss rate.
Teleoperation module may become too large and require further decomposition. Mitigation: plan sub-level decomposition into cohesive sub-modules.
Quality monitoring may generate high-frequency telemetry and create bottleneck. Mitigation: aggregate and filter quality metrics before publishing.
evidence_needed: Network topology and capacity analysis for agreed network conditions (e.g., 4G/5G coverage, latency, bandwidth).
Latency budget specification for teleoperation (e.g., max round-trip latency for command-to-feedback cycle).
FEC algorithm selection and parameter tuning (e.g., code rate, block size, overhead cap).
Redundancy strategy specification (e.g., dual-path routing, failover logic, path selection criteria).
Quality monitoring thresholds and alerting logic (e.g., loss rate thresholds, alert latency budget).
Operator feedback interface specification (e.g., quality indicator, codec status, failover notification).
Safety Services integration specification (e.g., SafetyAlert format, latency budget, emergency shutdown trigger).
Sub-level decomposition plan for Teleoperation module (e.g., Redundancy Manager, FEC Codec, Quality Monitor, Payload Optimizer).
